` ---
title: "Midterm project"
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_document: default
date: "2025-10-21"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
```
```{r}
stock <- read_csv("World-Stock-Prices-Dataset.csv")
head(stock)
```
## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r}
#install.packages("lubridate")
library(lubridate)
stock$Month <- format(stock$Date, "%Y-%m")
```
Wrangling process 

```{r}
stock_prices <- subset(stock, Month > 2016-01)
head(stock_prices)
monthly_price <- stock_prices %>% 
  group_by(Brand_Name, Month, Industry_Tag) %>% 
  summarize(monthly_price = mean(Close,na.rm =T))
head(monthly_price)
```
First plot

```{r, fig.width=8, fig.height=10}
par(mar=c(10, 4, 4, 2) + 0.1)
profit <- monthly_price %>% 
  filter(Month %in% c("2015-01", "2025-07")) %>% 
  group_by(Brand_Name, Industry_Tag) %>% 
  summarize(Start_Price = round(monthly_price[Month == "2015-01"], 2),
    End_Price = round(monthly_price[Month == "2025-07"], 2),
    Profit_Rate = round(((End_Price / Start_Price) - 1) * 100, 2))
head(profit)
ggplot(data = profit, aes(x = Brand_Name, y = Profit_Rate)) + 
  geom_point(col = "darkblue", size = 2) + coord_flip() + labs(
    title = "Profit Rate of Each Company", x = "Company Name", y = "Profit Rate")
```
Second plot

```{r}
average_return <- profit %>% group_by(Industry_Tag) %>% 
  summarise(average_return=mean(Profit_Rate, na.rm=T)) 
head(average_return)
par(mar=c(7, 4, 4, 2) + 0.1)
barplot(average_return$average_return, names.arg = average_return$Industry_Tag, 
    xlab = "", ylab = "Profit rate", main = "Average return by each industry",las = 2)
```
